# Quack

This repository is based on https://github.com/duckdb/extension-template, check it out if you want to build and ship your own DuckDB extension.

---

This extension, Quack, allow you to ... <extension_goal>.


## Building

### Build steps
Now to build the extension, run:
```sh
make
```
The main binaries that will be built are:
```sh
./build/release/duckdb
./build/release/test/unittest
./build/release/extension/quack/quack.duckdb_extension
```
- `duckdb` is the binary for the duckdb shell with the extension code automatically loaded.
- `unittest` is the test runner of duckdb. Again, the extension is already linked into the binary.
- `quack.duckdb_extension` is the loadable binary as it would be distributed.

## Running the extension
To run the extension code, simply start the shell with `./build/release/duckdb`.

Now we can use the features from the extension directly in DuckDB. The template contains a single scalar function `quack()` that takes a string arguments and returns a string:
```
D select quack('Jane') as result;
┌───────────────┐
│    result     │
│    varchar    │
├───────────────┤
│ Quack Jane 🐥 │
└───────────────┘
```

## Running the tests
Different tests can be created for DuckDB extensions. The primary way of testing DuckDB extensions should be the SQL tests in `./test/sql`. These SQL tests can be run using:
```sh
make test
```

### Installing the deployed binaries
To install your extension binaries from S3, you will need to do two things. Firstly, DuckDB should be launched with the
`allow_unsigned_extensions` option set to true. How to set this will depend on the client you're using. Some examples:

CLI:
```shell
duckdb -unsigned
```

Python:
```python
con = duckdb.connect(':memory:', config={'allow_unsigned_extensions' : 'true'})
```

NodeJS:
```js
db = new duckdb.Database(':memory:', {"allow_unsigned_extensions": "true"});
```

Secondly, you will need to set the repository endpoint in DuckDB to the HTTP url of your bucket + version of the extension
you want to install. To do this run the following SQL query in DuckDB:
```sql
SET custom_extension_repository='bucket.s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/<your_extension_name>/latest';
```
Note that the `/latest` path will allow you to install the latest extension version available for your current version of
DuckDB. To specify a specific version, you can pass the version instead.

After running these steps, you can install and load your extension using the regular INSTALL/LOAD commands in DuckDB:
```sql
INSTALL quack;
LOAD quack;
```

## Setting up CLion

### Opening project
Configuring CLion with this extension requires a little work. Firstly, make sure that the DuckDB submodule is available.
Then make sure to open `./duckdb/CMakeLists.txt` (so not the top level `CMakeLists.txt` file from this repo) as a project in CLion.
Now to fix your project path go to `tools->CMake->Change Project Root`([docs](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/clion/change-project-root-directory.html)) to set the project root to the root dir of this repo.

### Debugging
To set up debugging in CLion, there are two simple steps required. Firstly, in `CLion -> Settings / Preferences -> Build, Execution, Deploy -> CMake` you will need to add the desired builds (e.g. Debug, Release, RelDebug, etc). There's different ways to configure this, but the easiest is to leave all empty, except the `build path`, which needs to be set to `../build/{build type}`, and CMake Options to which the following flag should be added, with the path to the extension CMakeList:

```
-DDUCKDB_EXTENSION_CONFIGS=<path_to_the_exentension_CMakeLists.txt>
```

The second step is to configure the unittest runner as a run/debug configuration. To do this, go to `Run -> Edit Configurations` and click `+ -> Cmake Application`. The target and executable should be `unittest`. This will run all the DuckDB tests. To specify only running the extension specific tests, add `--test-dir ../../.. [sql]` to the `Program Arguments`. Note that it is recommended to use the `unittest` executable for testing/development within CLion. The actual DuckDB CLI currently does not reliably work as a run target in CLion.

cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.5)

set(TARGET_NAME jq)
set(EXTENSION_NAME ${TARGET_NAME}_extension)
set(LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME ${TARGET_NAME}_loadable_extension)

project(${TARGET_NAME})

set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD "17" CACHE STRING "C++ standard to enforce")
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)

include_directories(src/include)

# --- libjq (built from the ./jq submodule via its own autotools build) ---
include(ExternalProject)

set(JQ_SRC_DIR     ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/jq)
set(JQ_BUILD_DIR   ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/jq-build)
set(JQ_INSTALL_DIR ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/jq-install)
set(JQ_STATIC_LIB  ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/lib/libjq.a)

ExternalProject_Add(jq_external
  SOURCE_DIR        ${JQ_SRC_DIR}
  BINARY_DIR        ${JQ_BUILD_DIR}
  INSTALL_DIR       ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}
  CONFIGURE_COMMAND ${CMAKE_COMMAND} -E chdir ${JQ_SRC_DIR} autoreconf -i
            COMMAND ${JQ_SRC_DIR}/configure
                      --prefix=${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}
                      --disable-shared --enable-static --with-pic
                      --disable-maintainer-mode --disable-docs
                      --with-oniguruma=builtin
  BUILD_COMMAND      ${CMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM}
  INSTALL_COMMAND    ${CMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM} install
  BUILD_BYPRODUCTS   ${JQ_STATIC_LIB}
)

# Make headers visible at configure time (the dir exists after install; create
# it now so include_directories doesn't warn on first run).
file(MAKE_DIRECTORY ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include)

add_library(libjq STATIC IMPORTED GLOBAL)
add_dependencies(libjq jq_external)
set_target_properties(libjq PROPERTIES
  IMPORTED_LOCATION             ${JQ_STATIC_LIB}
  INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include
)

set(EXTENSION_SOURCES src/jq_extension.cpp)

build_static_extension(${TARGET_NAME} ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})
build_loadable_extension(${TARGET_NAME} " " ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})

# Link libjq in both the static library and the loadable extension
target_link_libraries(${EXTENSION_NAME}          libjq)
target_link_libraries(${LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME} libjq)

install(
  TARGETS ${EXTENSION_NAME}
  EXPORT "${DUCKDB_EXPORT_SET}"
  LIBRARY DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}"
  ARCHIVE DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}")

input.prompt:
I'm implementing a DuckDB scalar extension function in C++ that takes (JSON, VARCHAR) and where the VARCHAR argument must be a constant (foldable to a string at bind time, like a jq filter pattern). I need to find idiomatic patterns from the DuckDB source.

Please search the duckdb submodule at /Users/tim_vergenz/code/personal/duckdb-jq/duckdb/ and return:

1. Examples of bind_scalar_function_t implementations that:
   - Use `ExpressionExecutor::EvaluateScalar` to fold a constant argument to a Value
   - Throw `BinderException` / `InvalidInputException` if a non-foldable argument is provided
   - Return a custom `FunctionData` subclass that stores the folded string
   
   Look in src/function/scalar/ for examples. The `regexp_matches` / `regexp` functions and `like_escape` are good examples (regex pattern must be a constant). Also check `strftime` (format string constant).

2. The pattern for retrieving FunctionData from `ExpressionState` inside a scalar_function_t — look for `ExecuteFunctionState` and `BoundFunctionExpression::bind_info`.

3. How JSON values (LogicalType::JSON()) are stored — are they stored as VARCHAR / string_t at the storage layer? Look at any JSON extension scalar function that reads a JSON column. The json extension lives at /Users/tim_vergenz/code/personal/duckdb-jq/duckdb/extension/json/ or similar.

4. Examples of UnaryExecutor::Execute with string_t input and string_t output, where the output string is built from a heap-allocated buffer (e.g., `StringVector::AddString(result, c_string)` or `StringVector::AddStringOrBlob`).

For each, give me:
- File path + line numbers
- A short (5-15 line) code excerpt showing the pattern
- One-line explanation of what to copy

Keep response under ~600 lines total.

add_dependencies(libjq jq_external)

  IMPORTED_LOCATION             ${JQ_STATIC_LIB}
  INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES ${JQ_INSTALL_DIR}/include
)

set(EXTENSION_SOURCES src/jq_extension.cpp)

build_static_extension(${TARGET_NAME} ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})
build_loadable_extension(${TARGET_NAME} " " ${EXTENSION_SOURCES})

# Link libjq in both the static library and the loadable extension
target_link_libraries(${EXTENSION_NAME}          libjq)
target_link_libraries(${LOADABLE_EXTENSION_NAME} libjq)

install(
  TARGETS ${EXTENSION_NAME}
  EXPORT "${DUCKDB_EXPORT_SET}"
  LIBRARY DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}"
  ARCHIVE DESTINATION "${INSTALL_LIB_DIR}")

content.text:
Implementation complete. Summary of what was done in `src/jq_extension.cpp`:

- **`JqBindData`** (FunctionData subclass) — stores the constant filter string; `Copy`/`Equals` are filter-based so DuckDB can dedupe plans.
- **`JqScalarBind`** — rejects non-foldable / NULL / parametrized second args (`InvalidInputException` / `ParameterNotResolvedException`), folds via `ExpressionExecutor::EvaluateScalar`, then compiles the filter once at bind time so syntax errors surface during planning, not per row.
- **`JqInitLocalState`** + **`JqLocalState`** — each thread gets its own `jq_state` with the filter pre-compiled, so concurrent execution doesn't share jq's mutable state.
- **`JqScalarFun`** — `UnaryExecutor::ExecuteWithNulls` over the JSON column; per row: `jv_parse_sized` → `jq_start`/`jq_next` drain loop → 0 results becomes SQL NULL, 1 result is returned as-is, multiple results are wrapped in a JSON array; output written via `StringVector::AddString`.

Also fixed `CMakeLists.txt` to link `libonig.a` (jq's builtin oniguruma) — without it the loadable extension failed to link.

Smoke tests pass: scalar, array (`.b[]`), missing field (jq `null`), zero-result (SQL NULL via `empty`), bind-time syntax error, and non-constant-filter rejection.

The error message suggests both my registered function candidates are being rejected, which points to a type comparison issue.

The simplest fix is to just register the function with VARCHAR input instead. JSON columns implicitly cast to VARCHAR anyway since they're stored as VARCHAR underneath, and string literals naturally bind to VARCHAR, so this should work cleanly without needing to debug the alias behavior further.

content.text: The two signatures are deduplicating because `LogicalType::operator==` ignores aliases. The cleanest fix is to just accept `VARCHAR` as the input type — JSON values automatically downcast to VARCHAR since they share the physical type. We can keep returning `JSON` so downstream type info is preserved.

content.text:
All 12 assertions pass. The test file at `test/sql/jq.test` now covers everything from the smoke run:

- single-value filter (`.b[1]`)
- multi-result filter wrapped in array (`.b[]`)
- missing field (`.missing` → JSON `null`)
- jq builtin function (`ascii_upcase` — exercises the oniguruma link)
- zero results → SQL NULL (`empty`)
- per-row application over a JSON column
- bind-time error on invalid filter syntax (`.[`)
- bind-time error when the filter isn't a constant expression

One small companion change: I also updated `LoadInternal` to register the function as `(VARCHAR, VARCHAR) → JSON` instead of `(JSON, VARCHAR) → JSON`. JSON columns implicitly cast to VARCHAR (since `JSON` is `VARCHAR` with an alias) but the reverse isn't true, so the VARCHAR signature accepts both kinds of inputs without forcing callers to cast. Return type stays `JSON` so downstream consumers see the right type.
